feedback.text: To whom it may concern, Thank you for opening Digital Fairness Act for public consultation. In general, this is a very worthy initiative and i think it should be broader in its scope. 1. Legal protections against destroying video games. In this Act, could we please include legal protections against destroying video games by their publishers? An ECI Stop Destroying Video Games garnered more than 1,440,000 signatures, so EU citizens are concerned about this issue. That initiative called for game developers to leave their games in functional state once dev supper for these products end. A while ago I purchased a game called The Crew, which I used to play from time to time. However, I cant play it anymore because Ubisoft (the publisher) switched off the master server of this game. Since, to play this single-player game (which I bought with my own money) I have to connect with master server first (for authentication), this move essentially killed the game. Let that sink in: I (and many others) have bought a single-player game with no stated expiration date and one day the publisher decided to remove it from my (and others) possession. This is a severe anti-consumer practice which set an unwelcome precedent. 2. Unavailability of video game related digital-only content for purchase Another topic which could be addressed in this Act is the problem of DLCs (downloadable contents) for video games. As part of ongoing dev support, many modern games receive paid updates (DLCs) which unlock additional items, side quests, etc. in these games. These updates can often be only purchased online, whilst the base games are often sold as hard copies. Once the licensing deals for the game expires, that game has to be taken off the storefront (both digital and physical). New players can still find a hard copy of that game on used market, but they cant buy used DLCs for that game. These DLCs are permanently tied to people who purchased them. Used market for digital codes doesnt exist. Hence this is essentially locking out some players who are late to the party from accessing some vital content in these video games. This also means that many of those digital-only downloadable contents could become lost media in several decades, once all the original purchasers die without passing on their account login credentials. Thank you for reading my feedback on Digital Fairness Act. Yours sincerely, Marek Bieława
